questions.question: Explain the working of a 4:1 Multiplexer with its truth table and logic circuit.
keyPoints: A 4:1 MUX has 4 data inputs (I0,I1,I2,I3), 2 select lines (S1,S0), and 1 output (Y)
Truth table shows output equals selected input based on S1S0: 00→I0, 01→I1, 10→I2, 11→I3
Implementation requires 4 AND gates (one per input), 2 NOT gates (for select complements), and 1 OR gate
Boolean expression: Y = I0.S1'.S0' + I1.S1'.S0 + I2.S1.S0' + I3.S1.S0

questions.question: How many select lines are needed for a 64:1 Multiplexer? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: For 2^n inputs, n select lines are required
For 64 inputs: 2^n ≥ 64
Since 2^6 = 64, we need 6 select lines
General formula: number of select lines = log2(number of data inputs)

questions.question: Describe how a MUX can be used to implement a boolean function.
keyPoints: MUX can implement any boolean function using select lines as function variables
Connect function variables to select lines of a MUX
Determine output for each select combination from function truth table
Connect 0 or 1 to data inputs based on function output for that combination
No extra logic gates needed - MUX acts as universal logic generator

questions.question: Explain the difference between Multiplexer and Demultiplexer.
keyPoints: MUX: multiple inputs → single output (data selection)
DEMUX: single input → multiple outputs (data distribution)
Both use select lines for routing decisions
MUX acts as data selector, DEMUX acts as data distributor
Example: MUX combines phone calls, DEMUX separates them at destination

questions.question: Design a 8:1 MUX using two 4:1 MUXes and one 2:1 MUX.
keyPoints: Two 4:1 MUXes handle first 8 inputs (0-3 and 4-7)
Each 4:1 MUX uses 2 select lines (S1,S0) for internal selection
The 2:1 MUX uses third select line (S2) to choose between outputs of two 4:1 MUXes
When S2=0, upper 4:1 MUX output passes; when S2=1, lower 4:1 MUX output passes
This cascaded arrangement implements 8:1 selection with 3 select lines

questions.question: What is an Enable input in a MUX? Explain its significance with an example.
keyPoints: Enable (EN) input controls MUX operation - when inactive, output is disabled
Active HIGH enable: EN=1 enables operation, EN=0 forces output to 0
Active LOW enable: EN=0 enables operation, EN=1 disables MUX
Used for cascading MUXes and creating larger systems
Enables/disables entire MUX without affecting other circuit components

questions.question: Draw and explain the block diagram of a 2:1 Multiplexer.
keyPoints: 2:1 MUX has 2 data inputs (I0, I1), 1 select line (S), and 1 output (Y)
When S=0: Y = I0 (first input selected)
When S=1: Y = I1 (second input selected)
Boolean equation: Y = I0.S' + I1.S
Implementation requires 2 AND gates, 1 OR gate, 1 NOT gate

questions.question: Design a 4:1 Multiplexer using NAND gates only. Explain the design procedure.
keyPoints: Start with sum-of-products expression: Y = I0.S1'.S0' + I1.S1'.S0 + I2.S1.S0' + I3.S1.S0
Apply De Morgan's theorem to convert to NAND-only implementation
Create NAND gates for each product term
Use NAND gates as inverters where needed for complements
Final circuit uses multiple 2-input NAND gates arranged to achieve same functionality
Alternative: Use NAND gates as universal gates to implement any boolean expression
This approach reduces circuit complexity as NAND is a universal gate

questions.question: Implement a 16:1 Multiplexer using 4:1 Multiplexers. Show the block diagram and explain the cascading method.
keyPoints: A 16:1 MUX requires 4 select lines (since 2^4 = 16)
Use five 4:1 MUXes: four in first stage, one in second stage
First stage: Four 4:1 MUXes each handle 4 inputs (0-3, 4-7, 8-11, 12-15)
Each first-stage MUX uses select lines S1,S0 for internal selection
Second stage: One 4:1 MUX selects output from first stage MUXes using S2,S3
Total select lines: S3,S2 (for second stage) and S1,S0 (for first stage)
This hierarchical/cascaded approach reduces complexity compared to single-stage implementation

questions.question: Explain how a MUX can be used as a logic function generator. Implement the function F(A,B,C) = Σ(1,3,5,6) using an 8:1 MUX.
keyPoints: MUX can generate any boolean function by treating select lines as input variables
Connect function variables to MUX select lines
For each select combination, determine function output and connect to corresponding data input
For F(A,B,C) with minterms 1,3,5,6: use A,B,C as select lines S2,S1,S0
Connect 0 to I0,I2,I4,I7; connect 1 to I1,I3,I5,I6
No extra gates needed - MUX directly implements the function

questions.question: Compare MUX implementation using gates versus tri-state buffers. Explain advantages of each approach.
keyPoints: Gate-based: Uses AND-OR-NOT gates; all gates active simultaneously
Tri-state: Uses tri-state buffers where only one is active at a time
Tri-state advantages: Lower power consumption, simpler design for large MUXes, no contention issues
Gate-based advantages: More predictable timing, no bus contention, better for high-speed applications
Tri-state requires careful enable signal generation to prevent multiple outputs active
For larger MUXes, tri-state implementation is more common in PLDs and FPGAs
Gate implementation is traditional and easier to understand for educational purposes
